snp2.8:1.1:
Although often lost in translation, the overall structure of this poem is clearly articulated in the Pali.
The first seven verses—coming under the “because” (yasmā)—state reasons,
while the last verse, under the “so” (tasmā), draws the conclusion:
Find a good teacher and practice the Dhamma.
Because:
snp2.8:1.2: When you honor —as the devas, Indra— one from whom you might learn the Dhamma,
snp2.8:1.3: he, learned, honored, confident in you,
snp2.8:2.1: You, enlightened, heedful, befriending a teacher like that,
snp2.8:2.2: practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma, pondering, giving it priority,
snp2.8:3.1: But if you consort with a piddling fool
snp2.8:3.2: who’s envious, hasn’t come to the goal,
snp2.8:3.4: you’ll go to death without having cleared up the Dhamma right here, with your doubts unresolved.
snp2.8:4.1: Like a man gone down to a river—
snp2.8:4.3: and swept away in the current:
snp2.8:4.4: How can he help others across?
snp2.8:5.1:
Even so:
He who hasn’t cleared up the Dhamma,
snp2.8:5.2: attended to the meaning of what the learned say,
snp2.8:5.4: How can he get others to comprehend?
snp2.8:6.1: But as one who’s embarked on a sturdy boat,
snp2.8:6.3: would—thoughtful, skillful, knowing the needed techniques— carry many others across,
snp2.8:7.3: can get other people to comprehend—
snp2.8:7.4: when the conditions have arisen for them to lend ear.
snp2.8:8.1:
So:
You should befriend a person of integrity—
snp2.8:8.3: Practicing so as to know the goal,
snp2.8:8.4: when you’ve experienced the Dhamma, you get bliss.
